subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9! In this unit you become a data detective. Statistics is the part of mathematics that helps us collect information, record it neatly, show it in pictures, and then read those pictures to answer questions about the world. Every day people ask questions like ‘Which fruit do most children like?’ or ‘How many goats came to the well today?’ Statistics gives you the tools to answer questions like these carefully and fairly.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Ask a good survey question and collect data fairly from a group of people.
Record data quickly and neatly using tally marks and a frequency table.
Sort objects and numbers into groups using lists, tables and Venn diagrams.
Read a pictogram where one symbol stands for more than one item.
Make your own pictogram with a clear key.
Read and build a block graph (bar graph) with a title and labels.
Interpret data to answer questions: most, least, total, and how many more.
Choose the best way to show a set of data and explain your choice.

concepts.title: Collecting Data with a Good Question
concepts.explanation: Before you can draw any chart, you first need some data to draw. Data is simply information you gather. The most common way to gather data is a survey – you ask a group of people the same question and write down every answer. The very first step is to choose a clear question with a small set of answers to choose from. Imagine Amina wants to find out the favourite fruit of the children at Nuur Academy. A good question is: ‘Which of these fruits do you like best: mango, banana, papaya or dates?’ This is a good…
concepts.example: A good survey question is also fair. A fair question does not push people towards one answer. If Farah asked ‘You love football, don’t you?’ she is pushing people to say yes – that is not fair, and the data would be…

concepts.title: Recording Data with Tally Charts
concepts.explanation: When you ask people a question, the answers come quickly, one after another. You need a fast way to keep count without losing track. The best tool for this is the tally mark. A tally mark is a single straight line, and each line stands for one. You draw one line for every answer you hear. Tally marks have a clever trick for counting in fives. You draw four straight lines, and then the fifth line goes across the other four, like a little gate. This makes a bundle of five that is very easy to count. So |||| (four…
concepts.example: Let us record a real example. Khadija asks ten friends which drink they like best – camel milk, water, or juice – and hears these answers in order: camel milk, water, camel milk, juice, camel milk, water, camel milk,…

concepts.title: Sorting Data with Lists, Tables and Venn Diagrams
concepts.explanation: Data is much easier to understand when it is sorted into groups. There are several neat ways to sort. The simplest is a list. You can put a list in a sensible order – smallest to largest, or in alphabetical order. If Omar has the heights 125 cm, 118 cm and 129 cm, he can order them from shortest to tallest: 118 cm, 125 cm, 129 cm. A well-ordered list makes it easy to spot the smallest and the largest at a glance. A table sorts data into rows and columns, and it can hold more than one fact about each item. A table…
concepts.example: A Venn diagram is a special sorting tool made of circles. Each circle stands for one group. The clever part is that the circles overlap in the middle. An item that belongs to both groups goes in the overlap, which we…

concepts.title: Pictograms – Showing Data with Pictures
concepts.explanation: A pictogram is a chart that uses small pictures or symbols to show how many. Pictograms are friendly and colourful, and they make it very easy to see which group is the biggest just by looking. In a pictogram, each group has its own row, and you draw a symbol for the amounts. The longer the row of symbols, the more there are. The most important part of a pictogram is the key. The key tells you what one symbol is worth. Sometimes one symbol stands for just one item, but often one symbol stands for several items, to…
concepts.example: Let us read a pictogram with Zahra. It shows ice creams sold at a shop over three days, and the key says each 🍦 = 5 ice creams. Monday has 3 symbols, Tuesday has 5 symbols, and Wednesday has 2 symbols. To find each…

concepts.title: Block Graphs – Building Bars of Data
concepts.explanation: A block graph, also called a bar graph, shows data using blocks stacked into bars. Each group has its own bar, and the taller the bar, the more there are. Block graphs are excellent for comparing amounts at a glance, because your eyes can quickly see which bar is tallest and which is shortest. Every good block graph has some important parts. It has a title at the top that says what the graph is about, like ‘Favourite Colours’. It has labels along the bottom telling you what each bar stands for, such as red, blue,…
concepts.example: Let us read a block graph with Maryam. It shows favourite colours in her class. The red bar reaches 4, the blue bar reaches 10, the green bar reaches 8 and the yellow bar reaches 8. To answer ‘Which colour is most…

concepts.title: Interpreting Data – Reading the Story
concepts.explanation: Making a chart is only half the work. The real prize is interpreting it – reading the chart to answer questions and tell the story hidden in the numbers. Good data detectives ask the same few questions of every chart, whether it is a tally chart, a pictogram or a block graph. The first question is ‘Which group has the most?’ In a tally chart or table, this is the row with the biggest frequency. In a pictogram it is the longest row of symbols. In a block graph it is the tallest bar. The second question is the…
concepts.example: The third question is ‘How many altogether?’ To answer this you add every group's amount together. The fourth question is ‘How many more does one group have than another?’ To answer this you subtract the smaller amount…

methods.title: How to make a tally chart
methods.example: A tally chart shows mango as a gate of five and three more single marks. How many chose mango?
steps: Write each group in its own row.
Make one tally mark for every answer you hear.
Make every fifth mark a gate across the other four.
Count the marks to find each frequency, then check they add to your total.

methods.title: How to read a pictogram
methods.example: In a survey of 12 children, 5 chose banana and 4 chose papaya. The rest chose dates. How many chose dates?
steps: Read the key to see what one symbol is worth.
Count the symbols in the row.
Multiply the number of symbols by the key value.
For half a symbol, add half of the key value.

methods.title: How to read a block graph
methods.example: Sort these into ‘Even numbers’ and ‘Greater than 10’: 6, 9, 12, 18, 21. Which numbers go in the overlap?
steps: Read the title to see what the graph is about.
Read the scale up the side to see what each block is worth.
Find the top of the bar and read across to the scale.
Compare bars: the tallest is the most, the shortest is the least.

methods.title: Reading a pictogram
methods.example: A pictogram of books read has a key of 📚 = 10 books. Class B shows 6 symbols. How many books did Class B read?
steps: Solution: Each symbol is worth 10, and there are 6 symbols.
So 6 × 10 = 60 books.
Reading the key first told us each picture was worth 10, not 1.

methods.title: How many more in a pictogram
methods.example: Using the same key of 10, Class A shows 4 symbols and Class B shows 6 symbols. How many more books did Class B read?
steps: Solution: Class A = 4 × 10 = 40 books.
Class B = 6 × 10 = 60 books.
The difference is 60 − 40 = 20 more books for Class B.

methods.title: Reading a block graph
methods.example: A block graph shows goals scored: Lions 8, Tigers 12, Bears 6, Eagles 10. Which team scored the most, and how many did all teams score together?
steps: Solution: The tallest bar is the Tigers at 12 goals, so they scored the most.
For the total, add every bar: 8 + 12 + 6 + 10 = 36 goals altogether.
Check the reading a block graph result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Reading tally marks
workedExamples.prompt: A tally chart shows mango as a gate of five and three more single marks. How many chose mango?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A gate is 5, and three single marks add 3 more. So 5 + 3 = 8 children chose mango. The gate lets us count in fives, which is quicker than counting eight single lines.

workedExamples.title: Completing a frequency table
workedExamples.prompt: In a survey of 12 children, 5 chose banana and 4 chose papaya. The rest chose dates. How many chose dates?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: So far 5 + 4 = 9 children are counted. The survey had 12 children in all, so the dates group must be 12 − 9 = 3 children. Always use the total to check the parts add up correctly.

workedExamples.title: Sorting in a Venn diagram
workedExamples.prompt: Sort these into ‘Even numbers’ and ‘Greater than 10’: 6, 9, 12, 18, 21. Which numbers go in the overlap?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The overlap holds numbers that are both even and greater than 10. Check each: 12 is even and greater than 10 – yes. 18 is even and greater than 10 – yes. So 12 and 18 go in the overlap. (6 is even but not greater than 10; 21 is greater than 10 but not even; 9 is neither.)

workedExamples.title: Reading a pictogram
workedExamples.prompt: A pictogram of books read has a key of 📚 = 10 books. Class B shows 6 symbols. How many books did Class B read?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Each symbol is worth 10, and there are 6 symbols. So 6 × 10 = 60 books. Reading the key first told us each picture was worth 10, not 1.

workedExamples.title: How many more in a pictogram
workedExamples.prompt: Using the same key of 10, Class A shows 4 symbols and Class B shows 6 symbols. How many more books did Class B read?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Class A = 4 × 10 = 40 books. Class B = 6 × 10 = 60 books. The difference is 60 − 40 = 20 more books for Class B.

workedExamples.title: Reading a block graph
workedExamples.prompt: A block graph shows goals scored: Lions 8, Tigers 12, Bears 6, Eagles 10. Which team scored the most, and how many did all teams score together?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The tallest bar is the Tigers at 12 goals, so they scored the most. For the total, add every bar: 8 + 12 + 6 + 10 = 36 goals altogether.

workedExamples.title: Reading a scale that jumps in twos
workedExamples.prompt: On a block graph each block is worth 2 goats. Farah's bar is 5 blocks tall. How many goats does her bar show?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Each block is worth 2, and the bar is 5 blocks tall, so 5 × 2 = 10 goats. If we had forgotten the scale and said 5, we would have been badly wrong. Always read the scale first.

workedExamples.title: Choosing the best chart
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan wants to show which children play football AND also have a pet. Which sorting tool should he use, and why?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: He should use a Venn diagram, because it has an overlap that shows items belonging to both groups at once. A list or a single bar could not show the ‘both’ group clearly, but the intersection of a Venn diagram is made exactly for this.

workedExamples.title: How many children were surveyed in total
workedExamples.prompt: How many children were surveyed in total?
workedExamples.solution: Class A = 40, Class B = 60, Class C = 30 books.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: How many more children chose dog than chose rabbit?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A pictogram key says each 📚 = 10 books. Class A has 4 symbols, Class B has 6 symbols, Class C has 3 symbols. How many books did each class read?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Using the same pictogram, how many books were read by all three classes together?

activities.title: Family Favourites Survey
steps: Choose a fair question with four choices, such as ‘Which fruit do you like best: mango, banana, papaya or dates?’
Ask everyone in your family and use tally marks to record each answer.
Turn your tallies into a frequency table with a number for each fruit.
Add up all the frequencies and check the total matches how many people you asked.
Keep your frequency table – you will use it to build a chart in Activity 3.

activities.title: Tally Marks Counting Game
activities.materials: a window or doorway where you can watch things go past.
steps: Choose three things to count, such as cars, goats and people.
Watch for ten minutes and make a tally mark each time you see one.
Remember to make every fifth mark a gate across the other four.
Count your gates in fives, then add the extra marks. Which did you see most of?

activities.title: Build a Pictogram
activities.materials: the frequency table from Activity 1, paper and colours.
steps: Choose a simple symbol for your topic, such as a small circle or a fruit shape.
Decide what each symbol is worth and write it in a key (for small numbers, one symbol = one).
Draw a row of symbols for each fruit, all the same size, matching your frequencies.
Show your pictogram to your family and ask them which fruit was the favourite.

activities.title: Block Graph with Household Objects
activities.materials: small objects such as bottle caps, stones or dry beans, and a flat table.
steps: Pick three or four groups to compare, such as spoons, cups and plates in your kitchen.
Count how many of each and lay the objects in straight columns, one object per block.
Line the columns up side by side so they look like the bars of a block graph.
Look along the tops of the columns: which is tallest (most) and which is shortest (least)?

activities.title: Venn Diagram Sorting Investigation
activities.materials: two loops of string or two drawn circles that overlap, and a pile of objects.
steps: Label one circle ‘Round’ and the other circle ‘Can roll’.
Take objects from around the house and decide where each one goes.
Put objects that are both round and can roll in the overlap in the middle.
Count how many objects are round altogether, remembering to include the overlap.

activities.title: Data Detective Family Game
activities.materials: your family or friends and the four detective questions.
steps: Show your family the pictogram or block graph you made earlier.
Take turns asking the four detective questions: most, least, total, and how many more.
Give a point for each correct answer and see who becomes the top data detective.
Then let a family member ask you a tricky question about the chart.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand data best? Was it easier to read a pictogram or a block graph? What survey would you like to carry out next? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Tally Mark Rule
rules.text: One straight line means one. Every fifth mark goes across the group of four to make a gate of five. Count the gates in fives first, then add the extra single marks.

rules.title: Pictogram Key Rule
rules.text: Always read the key first. One symbol may be worth more than one item. Symbols times key value = the total. Half a symbol shows half the key value.

rules.title: Block Graph Scale Rule
rules.text: Read the scale up the side first. One block does not always mean one. The tallest bar shows the most; the shortest bar shows the least.

terms: Asking people a question to gather data
A line used to count one item
The overlap that belongs to both groups
What each block or step is worth
commonMistakes: Read the key and multiply the symbols
Read the scale, then count in that step
Missing the overlap in a Venn count
Include the overlap in each group's count
Counting tally marks one by one
Count gates in fives, then add the extras

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market, Sumaya tallies the fruit she sells: mango |||| ||||, papaya |||| , banana ||||. Write each total and say how many pieces of fruit she sold altogether.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Liban makes a pictogram of goats sold each day with each 🐐 = 5 goats. Monday 3 symbols, Tuesday 5 symbols, Wednesday 4 symbols. How many goats each day, and which day sold the most?
realProblems.answer: Monday 15, Tuesday 25, Wednesday 20; Tuesday sold the most.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

questions.question: Here is some data: 4, 8, 11, 18, 5, 4. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 4 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.question: Here is some data: 12, 8, 17, 5, 18. What is the largest value?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: A tally shows these counts: 7, 6, 17, 10, 19. What is the total?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Here is some data: 19, 16, 15, 17, 10, 19. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 19 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Here is some data: 3, 11, 6, 15, 16, 3. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 3 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.question: Here is some data: 17, 7, 5, 20, 6. What is the largest value?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What does “Tally mark” mean?

games.title: Quick Match: Data
games.description: Practise data through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: Here is some data: 4, 8, 11, 18, 5, 4. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 4 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 19 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

selfAssessment: I can ask a good survey question and collect data fairly from a group of people.
I can record data quickly and neatly using tally marks and a frequency table.
I can sort objects and numbers into groups using lists, tables and Venn diagrams.
I can read a pictogram where one symbol stands for more than one item.
I can make your own pictogram with a clear key.
I can read and build a block graph (bar graph) with a title and labels.
I can interpret data to answer questions: most, least, total, and how many more.
I can choose the best way to show a set of data and explain your choice.
